This is the opportunity to create a product, right? But that sometimes can be a little more complicated. So you can start any, that's why, I wrote it top to down just because that's how paper works and lists work, but you can really attack this any number of ways. And the best way to do it is try to fill in as many blanks as you can and see what's open. And that really will give you a hint of what you need to figure out in research or what you need to talk to or what else, where the potential big opportunity is. So yeah, sometimes we started out without a product, sometimes we were trying to sell a product. But usually we have the product itself, and Joe will get more into this when we talk about the offer. A strong product kind of generates big ideas and one that doesn't is probably a product we're not going to have around much longer. I got a comment about the idea of general idea, but in that book made a stick. I think one of the big points that came out of that to me was, don't think you have to come up with a big idea. I don't think I'm ever going to come up with a really, really good big idea because they're out there. You just have to go with it. So if you look at Scott's example, Canadian Social Security, he didn't come up with the fact that like, he didn't go to Canada and go in and say, what are you guys doing here? He's like, oh, you're trying to give it in, Scott's whatever. He probably read an article that just said, their Social Security program is better. That was the big idea. How did he find that? He was just reading. So it's a fact. It's tough to know what a big idea is anyway, but once you're at that point where you kind of know what big ideas are, don't think you have to come up with the final. So they're out there. And again, I don't know how to do this because copywriting is way harder for just playing copywriters. I've been through my life in a group of financial, I've been through different franchises and stuff. So I've been immersed by force almost. Like, I have to learn about what I have to learn about income. Those are the things. But in that process, being immersed in those things, you're just going to get hit over the head with big ideas. So if you find out where you're going to be, just reading for two weeks about Biotech, I guarantee I find a few big ideas or at least things that are close to big ideas that I can prioritize some sort of offer that makes it work. But the idea is you don't have to sit there. I think the template is a good idea. But I don't think you have to like, you're not going to, with this template in a room by yourself, you're, God bless you if you can come up with a big idea. You know, you got to go out there and try it. Someone else probably wrote it. It's trying to headline somewhere that they didn't tie to a publication. Or it's buried in an article somewhere and the author didn't know what he was doing with it. It's a big idea and it's out there. It's just a matter of researching the time. So you watch the Bachelor. And they're playing. Okay, so Caleb's our kind of guest star for today. I don't know how many of you guys know Caleb because he works off-site and he used to be in Virginia, but now he's down in Puerto Rico, lived in the good life, and he's way more tan than all of us will be in the wintertime here. But I'll tell you a quick story about how I met Caleb and kind of built Caleb up a little bit in a way that I know he won't do it for a very long time. A couple years ago, I was trolling the internet doing something and I came across one of these few to banner ads, and it showed like a moving gift for something I don't know what these things are called. I think that's the technical name of this AR-15 part. And it said something like, gun grabbers hate this man. And I was like, okay, that's an interesting banner. So I click on it and I go to a video sales letter and I'm watching. I'm like, damn, this is really good. And it was all about how you can build your own AR-15 completely legal but completely off the books with no serial number and no registration. And it was a whole BSL just like we have video sales letters. So I'm watching, watching. I'm like, damn, this is really good copy. It's a brilliant idea. I've never seen this idea before. So I went ahead and I bought the product and then I was like, you know what? Not only am I interested in this product, I'd like to do business with this guy. So who is this guy behind this thing? And he signed, I can say all the real names and the... He signed the end of the promotion Caleb Lee. So I'm like, okay, so I start googling Caleb Lee. I'm like, I can't find anything about this guy. So then I started googling Caleb and I started googling the customer service address that I got on my confirmation letter. And I found this thing called Caleb Osborn Consulting with the same customer service address as Caleb Lee on underground assault rifle, which was the product name. So I wrote to Caleb and I think it was like a, probably the weirdest email ever. I'm like, hey, I just bought your product and I'm kind of in this business too. I'd like to do some business with you. So give me a call or something. And I think he ended up writing me back saying, how in the world did you actually find me? Because I don't put my name on things. So Caleb and I talked for a while and I'm like, man, I really, really like your product. I like everything that you did. I'm like, how big is your company? And he's like, well, I'm just me. I'm like, what do you mean you're just me? And you were just like, I'm just me. I'm like, well, who laid out your video sales letter? He's like me. I'm like, you laid out the video sales letter. Yeah, yeah, who's buying your media? Who bought that banner? Me. Who wrote the copy? Me. I'm like, so you're doing everything? He's like, I do everything. So I'm like, damn, that's like super, super impressive. I was like, you want to write a copy for us? He's like, no. I was like, I don't know. I don't know. I don't know what I'm writing for you guys. So he's like, but I got a friend that would run and write a copy with you guys. So anyway, Caleb and I stayed in touch and eventually he got to the point, you know, like, I respect Caleb so, so, so much. Caleb does stuff that I would never be able to do. He is a one man shop. But even to this day, pretty much, a one man shop. He writes copy. I think brilliant. He has a way more technical background than I, you know, will ever have as far as laying out a video sales letter and putting it online on how to do any of that. He hopes up shopping carts, deals with merchant accounts, charge backs, everything like that Caleb does. But at some point, you know, Caleb said, hey, you know, maybe we can do some business together. Maybe we should write some promos together. Caleb came on and wrote the big drop copy, which did, I don't know, 25, 30,000 million or something like that minus 40% because it was a book offer. Anyway, I'll confuse you if I keep coming back. But then he also wrote retirement, the ultimate retirement loophole, which was kind of the first iteration of that David Blaine, you know, let's go wow people in the street by creating instant income type of thing. So Caleb's here from Puerto Rico. Today and tomorrow. So it's like a whole, you know, young kid focused thing like so easy, you know, an eight year old on the playground can do it. So we're going to film that tomorrow. But today we've got them. He's kind of here, you know, for us all today. So you might stand out here. Thanks. Hello. So I don't really, I don't really have much more to add or, you know, as a basis than what he said. So they were sold out of stores. You know, so in one of these templates, it says something about, you know, you wish you had come up with the idea yourself, or if you see something like, man, I would really buy that, you know, and you, especially if you were kind of your target market, or you know that there's a target market that's like you, then that's usually a general indication, you know, and everything he was saying, you know, when you tell somebody or you think about it, you instantly get that feeling like, man, this is a really sellable idea. And that's pretty much it. That was more of a happy circumstance, you know, but most of the time you have to do, you have to do a ton of research, you know, and a ton of reading. And that's really all you can do. And then generally, you'll find that one little piece of information that kind of sparks a big idea, you know, like the examples we were looking at, Canadian Social Security. That's, I guarantee he was reading that and then found out, you know, oh wow, the Canadian Social Security actually invest in stocks and then, you know, from there, you put the whole thing together. But yeah, that's pretty much how I've gotten to anything. Thank you. I have a question. So when you came up, when you found this stuff on the internet, you wrote forums and stuff, and you decided, well, I would buy that product. No. And get a feedback saying, oh yeah, I get that too. No. Just go on and just do it. Yeah, I just had kind of a feeling. So at the time, I already had one other, my first business was a weight loss e-book that I wrote myself. And then weight loss and exercise is even worse, right, because diet and exercise is how you lose weight, right? There's no real secret. But so you have to have a big idea. You have to have a unique mechanism. You have to have something to make your product unique, right? Because we're all selling the same information. So anyways, and then I came across that and I was really interested and I knew that the political climate at the time, I just had that feeling that would work. Yeah, so I didn't even run it by anybody. What I did is, I was like, well, I think this is a big idea. So I just, I filmed myself doing it when I wanted to do it, right? So then I could justify buying all the parts anyways and all that to my wife and everything. And then I filmed myself and I was like, I'll just throw it up and make the most basic product possible, you know, write the copy and then if it's got legs, I'll take it from there. And then it did. So that's how I did that. So the feedback was immediately getting into market as quick as possible, you know, which is probably something you can only do as a one man shop, you know. So yeah, that's that's how I tested that. I talked on Tuesday about the idea of like a copywriter, not just being a copywriter. You know, the idea that a copywriter needs to be this more holistic marketer now than what they didn't have to be 30 years ago. So again, if you guys remember the conversation, 30 years ago, a copywriter just writes something, goes away, can write for a different client, hands it over to a marketing manager and then a marketing manager takes 30 pages, you know, and basically just rents direct mail lists. Like the game wasn't that hard. Now the game is way more difficult because you got to stay compliant on networks. You got to figure out how you get traffic. You got to figure out how you, you know, do email drops in a way that gets you clicks to get to your promo and things like that. Caleb is, you know, the model of a holistic marketer. And you always told me something that when we first talked that thought was really interesting. I don't really want to be a copywriter. I know that's what I have to be to make the money. But you're like by default, I think you said, you're like, I don't think of myself as a copywriter. Like you're a marketer who uses copy because you need to to sell your product. So again, that's what we're going to do over the course of these 16 sessions. It's not just going to be copy. What we get later is going to be marketing ideas. I told you guys, like I want to make you guys, you know, better media buyers than most people that are out there. I want to make you better at recognizing good graphic design as far as it relates to direct response than graphic designers out there. And that's what Caleb is. I mean, Caleb designs his own shit and he designs his own stuff better than most graphic designers that we would ever think about. Like because he's thinking about it from a sales side. That he's buying his own media. He's buying all that banner traffic. He's buying all that stuff. So again, to compete with someone like Caleb, that's what we all have to learn, right? It's not just I'm going to write 30 pages and hand it over to the marketing team and forget about it. You need to be this more holistic, well-rounded marketer that's focused and specialized in copy. But you need to understand the rest of those pieces. To speak to that, something to make that really applicable for what we do here is if you know that you're writing a front end promotion versus a back end promotion, front end stuff, you really should think about the traffic patterns, you know, all the banners, all the emails, all the text ads, whatever. And if you can think about that from the beginning, you know, then you can kind of like come up with mechanisms and ideas within. That'll help you kind of maybe come up with a big idea or at least when you write the copy, have those mechanisms and things that could be featured in the traffic drivers. Like in the weight loss, I don't know, 10 years ago or whatever, it was like one simple trick to lose weight. It was like the dumbest, simplest little banners, right? But that was, if you look at what's working on driving traffic, then those are big ideas, right? If you can expand on those on the landing page because that's like the ultimate form of testing, right? It goes out to the most amount of people. So if you can kind of come up with mechanisms and pump your promo, especially with front end promotions, because we're going out into the general world and we're looking for eyeballs, you know, if you can drill down your big ideas to a really simple concept like that, that's really going to help on the front end stuff. Like, can the social security system be possible to ignore? You have to figure out what that is. And the back end, I mean, more applicable, I've learned the most from Ryan about the back end stuff. And it has to be very simple, very direct, and very quick to the money, you know? And then on that becomes, I think the mechanism on the back end, that's always going to be the promise. Like, it's going to be really quick, really big gains, you know, make a lot of money. And then the mechanism becomes more important. And maybe that speaks to what you were wondering about with the back end stuff, like the income on demand promotion that I had. My whole idea was just, well, let's say people record their, it's such a simple concept, because I don't really understand a lot about financial stuff anyways when I was reading it. I was like, oh, even I understand this, right? If we could just like videotape, have people videotape their brokerage accounts doing the trade, people will say, oh, easy, it's like 10 clicks. Yeah, that's what we did. So that was like the, you know, don't tell people, you know, show people, or demonstrate the product, kind of like they're doing it in commercials. So that's where the idea came from. So I think that's on the back end, you know, the promise is always going to be about the same. Okay, so we're almost out of time. So a couple quick wrap up notes. And I'm going to say this each and every single time. Three minimum requirements. Number one, you have to write down an idea a day. There's the sheet. I don't know how to say it anymore. It's the most important thing. Write down an idea a day. Because again, not every idea you're going to come up with is going to be a big idea. We're going to ask that at the end of these two months that we all go through this, you should have 60 ideas. Again, Saturday and Sunday both have the words day in them. You should write down an idea every single day, Saturday, Sunday, right? So you're going to have 60 ideas. You're going to throw 59 of those away and write up one. That's it. So if you only write down five ideas, that's not good. And you're going to have to choose one of those five to write up in the end of that 60 ideas. Write down 60 ideas. Number two, read a promotion a day. Again, actively read that promotion. Just like we were talking about before the copy camp started. And number three, write a page a day. So let's be honest. Who didn't write a page a day yesterday? Okay, Andy did. Andy's the only fucking honest one in this room. Everybody else fucking wrote a page a day yesterday. Everybody else in this room wrote a page. What's that? I did write an app page of round and odd. Two semi-honest people in this room. Anyway, those are three things. Write down an idea a day. Read a promo a day. Write a page a day. No matter who you are, write a page a day. A page a day. Improve someone else's idea. Write down a page about a new offer you want to do. Write down a page as far as a video demonstration. Minimum. Minimum. Minimum. If you have four ideas a day, write down four ideas. If you just feel like that spark and you want to keep writing, keep writing. But those are just the minimums. And do what I do, which is if I have four ideas in a day, I'm going to make one of those ideas for tomorrow, one of those ideas for the next day. Because at some point I'm going to be lazy and forget. But then I know that I'm going to front load ideas enough that I'll always show up on that sheet with an idea a day.
